content: A black hole is a solution to Einstein’s field equations characterized by a region of spacetime from which causal signals cannot escape to asymptotic infinity. Typically described by metrics such as Schwarzschild or Kerr, its defining feature is the event horizon—a null hypersurface delineating the boundary of no return. Within this boundary, the light cones tip inward so profoundly that all future-directed null and timelike geodesics converge toward the singularity. This causal structure implies that the escape velocity locally exceeds the speed of light, forbidding any information or matter from traversing outward across the horizon. Consequently, the event horizon acts not merely as a gravitational barrier but as a one-way causal membrane, rendering the interior region causally disconnected from the external universe. The black hole’s inescapability thus arises from the global geometry of spacetime rather than any force, embodying a fundamental limit on information propagation dictated by relativistic causality.
